review_candidates.comment: Every killing cited is grounded in events: Duncan (E_duncan_killed — kinsman per E_macbeth_kinsman_of_duncan, king per E_duncan_king_of_scotland, guest per E_duncan_visits), Banquo (E_banquo_killed, ordered by Macbeth), Lady Macduff and son (E_macduff_family_killed, ordered by Macbeth). The 'hero of Act 1' claim is supported by E_macbeth_defends_scotland. The trajectory reading is well-grounded and internally consistent.

Each prophecy claim maps to events: 'Thane of Cawdor' → E_thane_of_cawdor_awarded, 'Will be king' → E_macbeth_crowned, 'none of woman born' → E_macduff_reveals_birth (born_not_of_woman(macduff)), 'Birnam Wood moves' → E_birnam_moves. The Caesarean-birth gloss is a reasonable interpretive expansion of born_not_of_woman(macduff). The framing that prophecies are literally true but that Macbeth's interpretations fail is cleanly supported by the event sequence.

The description accurately characterizes the encoding: E_prophecy_first and E_prophecy_second place prophecy content exclusively in agent_knowledge slots (known via utterance-heard), with no world-facts asserting foresight. The question is well-posed and the three candidate ontologies (supernatural, manipulative, projective) are legitimate options the encoding currently avoids choosing among.

E_banquet_ghost records the apparition only as an agent_knowledge effect on macbeth, with no world-level assertion that the ghost is present. The description correctly notes the structural parallel to the Witches' encoding. The question is well-framed and identifies a genuine, deliberate gap in the substrate.

Two claims overreach the events. First, 'her suicide' — E_lady_macbeth_dies is type 'death' with no cause specified; calling it suicide is an ungrounded attribution. Second, 'precedes his death by the smallest possible interval' — her death is at τ_s=14, his at τ_s=17, with E_birnam_moves (τ_s=15) and E_macduff_reveals_birth (τ_s=17) between them; three τ_s units and multiple events is not the smallest possible interval. The core inversion argument is strong and well-supported.

The rules are verifiable against the event base: killed(macbeth,duncan) ∧ kinsman_of(macbeth,duncan), killed(macbeth,duncan) ∧ king(duncan,scotland), killed(macbeth,duncan) ∧ guest_of(duncan,macbeth) all hold. The tyrant depth-2 derivation chains correctly. The acknowledged gap — that ordered_killing(macbeth, lady_macduff/macduff_son) per E_macduff_family_killed does not yet factor into tyrant — is explicitly flagged for follow-on work.

The description references 'the "tomorrow" soliloquy removes the capacity to feel' but no event in the view encodes this soliloquy. The trajectory argument's other anchor-points (Duncan's killing, Banquo's killing, Macduff-family slaughter) are all grounded in events; this one is not. The structural point about trajectory-pattern verification versus single-moment verification is sound.

proposed_description.text: The encoding's ontological reticence about the Witches should be preserved as an explicit, permanent commitment rather than treated as a gap awaiting resolution. The event structure records prophecy content as agent_knowledge effects on macbeth and banquo (via utterance-heard) without any world-fact asserting foresight or causal power — and this is the right representation. The play supports at least three readings (genuine supernatural foresight, self-fulfilling manipulation, externalized ambition), and collapsing to one would lose interpretive range that the source material deliberately sustains. A formal marker such as ontology_of(witches) = deliberately_uncommitted would elevate this reticence from an implicit absence to a principled modeling decision. If a future branch wishes to explore a committed ontology, it can do so as a :contested branch (e.g., :b-witches-supernatural adding world-facts for foresight), leaving :canonical uncommitted.

answer_proposals.rationale: E_prophecy_first and E_prophecy_second both use agent_knowledge effects exclusively, never asserting world-facts about the Witches' nature. The description correctly identifies this as a structural choice worth formalizing rather than resolving.

proposed_description.text: The ghost's agent-only encoding should be maintained as a deliberate parallel to the Witches' ontological reticence. E_banquet_ghost records the apparition as an agent_knowledge effect on macbeth alone — no other participant gains knowledge of the ghost, and no world-fact asserts its presence. This mirrors the dramatic structure: the ghost is perceptible only to Macbeth, making it structurally ambiguous between visitation and hallucination. Committing a world-fact (ghost-is-real) would invalidate the guilt-projection reading; committing to hallucination-only would lose the supernatural resonance with the Witches' prophecies. The two ontological questions (Witches, ghost) should be resolved — or left unresolved — as a pair, since the substrate treats them with the same pattern and they reinforce each other's interpretive openness.

answer_proposals.rationale: E_banquet_ghost places the apparition effect only on macbeth's agent_knowledge, with no world-level assertion. This encoding pattern is structurally identical to E_prophecy_first's treatment of the Witches, as D_witches_ontology_undecided already notes.

proposed_description.text: The RESOLVED status is well-earned: the four inference rules correctly derive from base facts present in the view. kinslayer(macbeth, duncan) follows from E_duncan_killed + E_macbeth_kinsman_of_duncan; regicide(macbeth, duncan) from E_duncan_killed + E_duncan_king_of_scotland; breach_of_hospitality(macbeth, duncan) from E_duncan_killed + E_duncan_visits. The depth-2 tyrant(macbeth) derivation chains through kinslayer and regicide plus E_macbeth_crowned. The acknowledged gap around the tyrant rule is substantive: E_macduff_family_killed records ordered_killing(macbeth, lady_macduff) and ordered_killing(macbeth, macduff_son), but these facts do not contribute to the tyrant derivation. A candidate refinement for inference-02 would be: ordered_killing(X, Y) ∧ innocent(Y) ∧ king(X, _) ⇒ tyrant(X), which would give the Macduff-family slaughter independent force in the derivation rather than leaving tyrant dependent solely on the Duncan killing's compound breaches.

answer_proposals.rationale: All base predicates consumed by the rules are verifiable in the event list. The gap concerning E_macduff_family_killed is explicit in the description and addressable once the substrate supports innocent-civilian tagging.

proposed_description.text: Lady Macbeth is the play's driving force in Act 1 and Act 2 — she reads the letter and decides; she chides Macbeth into the killing; she handles the daggers after. Then the arc reverses. Macbeth becomes the calculating one (Banquo, the Macduff family); Lady Macbeth becomes the one who cannot bear it. The sleepwalking is the external symptom of the inversion complete; her death at τ_s=14 precedes his at τ_s=17, the two endpoints nearly adjacent in the play's compressed finale. The Impact Character role's *direction* is not constant across the play; the role itself inverts.

edit_proposals.rationale: Changed 'her suicide' to 'her death' to match E_lady_macbeth_dies (type 'death', no cause asserted). Replaced 'smallest possible interval' with 'nearly adjacent in the play's compressed finale', which is defensible without overstating proximity given three τ_s units and intervening events.

proposed_description.text: Under dramatica-complete's DynamicStoryPoint(judgment=bad), Macbeth's Judgment is earned across the trajectory, not at any moment. Duncan's killing is the precipitation that drops him into the descent, but judgment=bad lands cumulatively: Banquo's killing removes the comradeship; the Macduff-family slaughter removes the pretext of political necessity; Lady Macbeth's death severs his last human attachment. A single-moment verifier at τ_s=17 (Macbeth's death) could confirm judgment=bad for the wrong reason (the MC ends dead). A trajectory-pattern verifier that reads the accumulating descent is the right check. This is the forcing case for lowering-sketch-02's F8.

edit_proposals.rationale: Replaced the 'tomorrow' soliloquy reference (no event in view encodes it) with Lady Macbeth's death (E_lady_macbeth_dies, τ_s=14), which is grounded in the events and preserves the escalating-loss structure of the trajectory argument.
